title: git_clone / git_pull / git rev-parse may execute via PATH-injected git binary
description:
## Severity
Major
## Dimension
security
## Location
src/plugins/git.rs:26-46, src/plugins/git.rs:55-71, src/plugins/git.rs:80-93
## Violation
All three subprocess invocations construct the git command with a bare program name. \"std::process::Command::new(\"git\")\" resolves \"git\" by walking the runtime PATH, so any directory writeable by an attacker that ends up earlier on PATH (e.g. a poisoned workspace, manipulated user shell, CI runner with misordered PATH) lets a malicious binary masquerade as git and run with the user's privileges during plugin install/update. Quoted lines:

    let mut cmd = std::process::Command::new(\"git\");
    cmd.arg(\"clone\").arg(\"--depth\").arg(\"1\");
    ...
    let output = std::process::Command::new(\"git\")
        .arg(\"rev-parse\").arg(\"HEAD\")
    ...
    let output = std::process::Command::new(\"git\")
        .arg(\"pull\")

Plugin install is an untrusted-code path, so PATH-injection escalation is a Major-severity supply-chain hole.
## Mandated refactor
Resolve git's absolute path once at process start using which::which (or equivalent) and cache the result in a module-level OnceLock; pass that PathBuf to Command::new throughout git.rs. Refuse install when the canonical git binary is not under a system-trusted prefix (e.g. /usr/bin) and a managed PluginPolicy is active.
## Filed from QA review dispatch
